Organizations Involved: American Medical Society for Sports Medicine
content.Committee Information: Joint Committee on Clinical Practice Guidelines
content.Publication Information: Published in the Journal of the American College of Cardiology, Vol. 83, No. 23, 2024
content.Approval: This document was approved by the American College of Cardiology Clinical Policy Approval Committee and the American Heart Association Science Advisory and Coordinating Committee in January 2024, and the American College of Cardiology Science and Quality Committee and the American Heart Association Executive Committee in February 2024.
content.Availability: Available on the websites of the American College of Cardiology and the American Heart Association

content.AIM: The "2024 AHA/ACC/AMSSM/HRS/PACES/SCMR Guideline for the Management of Hypertrophic Cardiomyopathy" provides recommendations to guide clinicians in the management of patients with hypertrophic cardiomyopathy.
content.METHODS: A comprehensive literature search was conducted from September 14, 2022, to November 22, 2022, encompassing studies, reviews, and other evidence on human subjects that were published in English from PubMed, EMBASE, the Cochrane Library, the Agency for Healthcare Research and Quality, and other selected databases relevant to this guideline. Additional relevant studies, published through May 23, 2023, during the guideline writing process, were also considered by the writing committee and added to the evidence tables, where appropriate.
content.STRUCTURE: Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy remains a common genetic heart disease reported in populations globally. Recommendations from the "2020 AHA/ACC Guideline for the Diagnosis and Treatment of Patients With Hypertrophic Cardiomyopathy" have been updated with new evidence to guide clinicians.

content.General Information: The document outlines updates to the 2024 Hypertrophic Cardiomyopathy (HCM) Guidelines, highlighting revised recommendations for heart rhythm assessment, exercise stress testing, ICD placement, and management of patients with HCM. Notable changes include recommendations for heart rhythm monitoring, considerations for ICD placement, management strategies for obstructive and nonobstructive HCM, and guidance on recreational physical activity and sports participation.

Key Messages: Shared decision-making is essential for optimal clinical care and involves discussions among patients, families, and healthcare teams regarding treatment options and personal preferences.
Referral to specialized hypertrophic cardiomyopathy (HCM) centers is essential for the management of patients, particularly for complex treatment decisions.
Family history and genetic testing are crucial for managing HCM, with the need for regular pathogenetic evaluation.
Risk assessment for sudden cardiac death is important, especially for decisions regarding implantable cardioverter-defibrillator placement.
The risk factors for sudden cardiac death in children differ from adults, and specialized management at HCM centers is recommended.
Cardiac myosin inhibitors such as Mavacamten are now available for symptomatic obstructive HCM.
Invasive septal reduction therapies are effective for drug-refractory cases.
Risk of atrial fibrillation in HCM patients necessitates careful management, with new tools aiding in risk assessment.
Exercise stress testing is valuable for identifying latent exercise-induced left ventricular outflow tract obstruction.
Regular structured exercise is beneficial for HCM patients and does not increase ventricular arrhythmias risk.

content.Preamble: The guidelines were developed by the American College of Cardiology (ACC) and American Heart Association (AHA) to promote cardiovascular health. They are based on systematic methods and provide a foundation for cardiovascular care delivery.
content.Intended Use: These guidelines offer recommendations applicable to patients with or at risk of cardiovascular disease, primarily focusing on medical practice in the United States.
content.Clinical Implementation: Effective management is achieved when recommendations are followed by practitioners and patients, emphasizing decision-making and patient engagement.
content.Guidelines Review: The ACC/AHA Joint Committee continuously reviews and updates guidelines based on published standards and internal reevaluation. Guidelines are restructured for user-friendliness.
content.New Data and Evidence: Guidelines are updated with new data reviewed by the writing committee. Sections are dynamically reviewed after publication, eliminating previous designations like 'full revision.'
content.Writing Committee: The committee consists of experts spanning various demographics and professional societies to ensure comprehensive expertise.
content.Relationships with Industry: The ACC and AHA have policies to prevent bias, ensuring transparency in relationships with industry and other entities.
content.Evidence Review: Development includes evidence-based methodologies with independent evidence review commissions for significant questions.

introduction.methodology: The guidelines are based on an extensive evidence review using literature from MEDLINE, EMBASE, Cochrane Library, and other databases from 2013 to 2022. The focus includes topics like hypertrophic cardiomyopathy, coronary issues, and diagnostic imaging.
introduction.writing_committee: The committee includes a diverse group of clinicians and lay representatives from organizations such as ACC, AHA, and Heart Rhythm Society.
introduction.document_review: A peer review process was employed involving a committee appointed by ACC and AHA.
introduction.scope: Develops the '2024 AHA/ACC/AMSSM/HRS/PACES/SCMR Guideline for the Management of Hypertrophic Cardiomyopathy' incorporating past guidelines.

section_2_1.title: Prevalence
section_2_1.details: HCM is a common inherited heart disease reported in populations globally. The estimated prevalence of HCM varies depending on whether subclinical or clinically evident cases are being considered, how or if the diagnosis is adjudicated, and age of the sample studied. The prevalence of unexplained asymptomatic hypertrophy in young adults in the United States has been reported in the range of 1:500. Symptomatic hypertrophy based on medical claims data has been estimated at <1:3000 adults in the United States; however, the true burden is much higher when unrecognized disease in the general population is considered. HCM is often inherited in an autosomal dominant pattern but does not require a family history of HCM. There is equal distribution of HCM by sex, although women are diagnosed less commonly than men. Differences in prevalence have been reported by race and ethnicity.

guideline_info.title: Applying American College of Cardiology/American Heart Association Class of Recommendation and Level of Evidence to Clinical Strategies, Interventions, Treatments, or Diagnostic Testing in Patient Care

class_of_recommendation.class_1: Strong. Benefit >>> Risk. Includes phrases like 'Is recommended' and 'Should be performed/administered/other'.
class_of_recommendation.class_2a: Moderate. Benefit >> Risk. Includes phrases like 'Is reasonable' and 'Treatment/strategy A is probably recommended'.
class_of_recommendation.class_2b: Weak. Benefit = Risk. Includes phrases like 'May/might be reasonable' and 'Usefulness/effectiveness is unknown/unclear'.
class_of_recommendation.class_3_no_benefit: Moderate. Benefit = Risk. Includes phrases like 'Is not recommended' and 'Should not be performed/administered/other'.
class_of_recommendation.class_3_harm: Strong. Risk > Benefit. Includes phrases like 'Potentially harmful' and 'Should not be performed/administered/other'.

level_of_evidence.level_a: High-quality evidence from more than 1 RCTs. Meta-analyses of high-quality RCTs.
level_of_evidence.level_b-r: Moderate-quality evidence from 1 or more RCTs. Meta-analyses of moderate-quality RCTs.
level_of_evidence.level_b-nr: Moderate-quality evidence from well-executed nonrandomized studies, observational studies, or registry studies.
level_of_evidence.level_c-ld: Randomized or nonrandomized observational or registry studies with limitations. Meta-analyses of such studies.
level_of_evidence.level_c-eo: Consensus of expert opinion based on clinical experience.

additional_info.importance_of_rct: RCTs are crucial but not always necessary if clinical consensus is clear.
additional_info.method_of_assessment: Including use of standardized and validated evidence grading tools.
additional_info.context: Epidemiological studies indicate the need to understand the interplay between genetics and social factors in HCM.

content.Clinical Context: The document focuses on hypertrophic cardiomyopathy (HCM), its clinical definition, diagnosis, and phenotype. It distinguishes HCM from other conditions that may mimic its symptoms, such as RASopathies, mitochondrial myopathies, glycogen storage diseases, Fabry disease, amyloid cardiomyopathies, and stress cardiomyopathy. The importance of genetic testing in diagnosing HCM and differentiating it from other conditions is emphasized.
content.Diagnostic Criteria: HCM diagnosis is primarily based on the imaging of the heart showing abnormal left ventricular thickness, specifically through 2D echocardiography or cardiovascular magnetic resonance. A maximal end-diastolic wall thickness of 15mm or more in adults is indicative of HCM.
content.Screening Recommendations: Asymptomatic children with a family history of HCM or a positive genetic test may be screened with a threshold of a z-score >2. Family members with pathogenic variants should be carefully monitored for early detection.
content.Morphological Features: Various morphological abnormalities are part of the phenotype, including hypertrophied papillary muscles, myocardial crypts, and anomalous mitral valve insertions. These features are not pathognomonic but part of the diagnostic criteria.
content.Genetic Basis: The section discusses the genetic components of HCM, identifying variants in known sarcomere genes linked to the condition. Approximately 30% to 60% of HCM patients have identifiable genetic variants.

genes.context: RASopathies (variants in several genes involved in RAS-MAPK signaling) can mimic the conditions of HCM.

content.summary: The guideline discusses genetic variants linked to Hypertrophic Cardiomyopathy (HCM) with some patients showing no evidenced genetic etiology, suggesting new pathophysiologic mechanisms. It highlights sarcomeric genes like MYH7 and MYBPC3 as common variants. Other genes like TNNI3, TNNT2, TPM1, MYL2, MYL3, and ACTC1 are mentioned with less frequency. HCM can lead to adverse events including morality, heart failure, and atrial fibrillation, with risk factors like obesity and diabetes being influential. Prevention includes risk stratification for sudden cardiac death and consideration of implantable devices.

content.Clinical Guideline: This clinical guideline discusses hypertrophic cardiomyopathy (HCM) with a focus on left ventricular outflow tract obstruction (LVOTO), diastolic dysfunction, mitral regurgitation (MR), myocardial ischemia, and autonomic dysfunction.
content.LVOTO: Obstruction is considered present if peak LVOT gradient is ≥30 mm Hg. Provocative maneuvers may be used to assess symptoms.
content.Diastolic Dysfunction: Altered ventricular load with high intracavitary pressures and impaired LV compliance can cause diastolic abnormalities in young sarcomere gene variant carriers.
content.Mitral Regurgitation: MR in HCM may be caused by systolic anterior motion (SAM) of the mitral valve, leading to a variety of potential symptoms.
content.Myocardial Ischemia: Patients may be susceptible to ischemia due to mismatch between oxygen supply and demand, often exacerbated by the presence of hyperdynamic systolic function.
content.Autonomic Dysfunction: Patients with HCM may experience autonomic dysfunction, with no specific recommendations for assessment.

Shared Decision-Making.Recommendation: For patients with HCM or at risk for HCM, shared decision-making is recommended in developing a plan of care including genetic evaluation, activity, lifestyle, and therapy choices with full disclosure of risks and benefits.
Shared Decision-Making.Synopsis: Shared decision-making involves patients, families, and health care professionals collaborating to consider the patient's input and preferences, improving confidence and outcomes.
Shared Decision-Making.Supportive Text: In HCM management, decisions about genetic testing, ICD implantation, advanced therapies for LVOTO, and exercise participation are crucial for discussions. Multidisciplinary HCM Centers provide opportunities for comprehensive experience.

Recommendations.Text: In patients with HCM where SRT is indicated, procedures should occur at experienced centers with clinical outcomes excellence.
For patients with HCM, consultation or referral to comprehensive or primary HCM centers is advisable for managing complex disease-related decisions.

Multidisciplinary HCM Centers.Synopsis: The evolving management of HCM calls for HCM centers with specific competencies. These centers optimize care for patients and offer roles for cardiologists both within and outside HCM centers.
Multidisciplinary HCM Centers.Supportive Text: Referral to HCM centers provides comprehensive management, genetic counseling, advanced treatment advice, and high-level longitudinal care. Inexperienced centers performing invasive SRT may result in low procedural volume.

content.diagnosis_evaluation: Clinical evaluation for HCM should start with a comprehensive cardiac history, including a family history of 3 generations. This is followed by a physical examination and specific diagnostic tests to confirm or rule out HCM.
content.recommendation: In patients with suspected hypertrophic cardiomyopathy (HCM), a thorough physical examination and a detailed medical and 3-generation family history are recommended.
content.screening: Screening with echocardiography is recommended in asymptomatic family members. The timing of screenings varies based on age and family history.

clinical_features.infants_and_toddlers: Dysmorphic features, failure to thrive in conditions like RASopathies, glycogen storage diseases.
clinical_features.early_childhood: Delayed or abnormal development related to RASopathies and mitochondrial diseases.
clinical_features.youth_and_adolescence: Skeletal muscle weakness, ataxia due to conditions like Friedreich’s ataxia.
clinical_features.adulthood: Movement disorder, neuropathy associated with diseases like Anderson-Fabry.

Echocardiography Recommendations.Recommendation: In patients with suspected HCM, a transthoracic echocardiogram (TTE) is recommended in the initial evaluation.

In patients with HCM who have no change in clinical status or events, repeat TTE is recommended every 1 to 2 years to assess the degree of myocardial hypertrophy, dynamic LVOTO, MR, and myocardial function.
For patients with HCM who experience a change in clinical status or a new clinical event, repeat TTE is recommended.
For patients with HCM and resting peak LVOT gradient <50 mm Hg, a TTE with provocative maneuvers is recommended.

For symptomatic patients with HCM who do not have a resting or provocable outflow tract peak gradient ≥50 mm Hg on TTE, exercise TTE is recommended for the detection and quantification of dynamic LVOTO.

For patients with HCM who are undergoing surgical septal myectomy, intraoperative transesophageal echocardiogram (TEE) is recommended to assess mitral valve anatomy and function and adequacy of septal myectomy.

For patients with HCM who are undergoing alcohol septal ablation, TTE or intraoperative TEE with intracoronary ultrasound-enhancing contrast injection of the candidate's septal perforator(s) is recommended.
For patients with HCM who have undergone SRT, TTE within 3 to 6 months after the procedure is recommended to evaluate the procedural results.
Screening: In first-degree relatives of patients with HCM, a TTE is recommended as part of initial family screening and periodic follow-up.

Screening: In individuals who are genotype-positive, phenotype-negative, echocardiography is recommended at periodic intervals depending on age (1-2 years in children and adolescents, 3-5 years in adults) and change in clinical status.

For patients with HCM, TEE can be useful if TTE is inconclusive in clinical decision-making regarding medical therapy, and in situations such as planning for myectomy, exclusion of subaortic membrane or MR secondary to structural abnormalities of the mitral valve apparatus, or in the assessment of the feasibility of alcohol septal ablation.

For patients with HCM in whom the diagnosis of apical HCM, apical aneurysm, or atypical patterns of hypertrophy is inconclusive on TTE, the use of an intravenous ultrasound-enhancing agent is reasonable, particularly if other imaging modalities such as CMR are not readily available or are contraindicated.

For asymptomatic patients with HCM who do not have a resting or provocable outflow tract peak gradient ≥50 mm Hg on standard TTE, exercise TTE is reasonable for the detection and quantification of dynamic LVOTO.

content.Synopsis: Cardiac imaging is critical for diagnosing and managing hypertrophic cardiomyopathy (HCM). Echocardiography is the main tool, with CMR as an adjunct. Imaging helps diagnose, assess severity and associated cardiac abnormalities, and risk stratification.
Recommendations: Comprehensive 2D echocardiography is primary for diagnosing HCM, assessing LV apical aneurysms, LV function, mitral valve function, and LVOTO severity.
Routine follow-up using TTE every 1 to 2 years in asymptomatic HCM patients is crucial to monitor changes.
Changes in HCM symptoms require thorough evaluation due to possible hemodynamic or valvular disease changes. Echocardiography is primary in symptom evaluation.
LVOT gradients are dynamic; provocative maneuvers can help detect LVOTO.
Effort-related symptoms attributed to LVOTO require specific criteria to diagnose.
Intraoperative TEE is standard for surgical guidance in HCM, assessing residual SAM and LVOTO.
TTE or TEE can help guide alcohol septal ablation with ultrasound-enhancing agents improving outcomes.
Post-SRT, evaluation of septal thickness and gradient decrease is recommended, acknowledging post-operative variability.
Following HCM diagnosis, first-degree relatives should undergo echocardiography screening.

content.pediatric studies yield: Pediatric studies show echocardiographic screening for clinical HCM in first-degree relatives was 10% to 15% throughout childhood and adolescence.
content.median age of HCM onset: Median age at HCM onset was 8.9 years in males.
content.family history and genetics: Particularly noted in those with family history, SCD, and pathogenic variants in MYH7/MYBPC3.
content.clinical screening: Regular screening is advised in childhood and adulthood for families with history of HCM.
content.TEE and HCM: TEE is useful in uncertain cases of mitral valve issues or valve stenosis.
CMR imaging recommendation: Suspected HCM when echocardiography is inconclusive.
LVH with suspicion of alternative diagnoses.
HCM not high risk for SCD needs CMR for detailed assessment.

genes.context: Particularly noted in those with family history, SCD, and pathogenic variants in MYH7/MYBPC3.
Particularly noted in those with family history, SCD, and pathogenic variants in MYH7/MYBPC3.

content.Synopsis: CMR imaging offers high spatial resolution for detecting and assessing myocardial replacement fibrosis (LGE). It's key for diagnosing, predicting risks in hypertrophic cardiomyopathy (HCM), and planning procedures. CMR imaging can quantify LV wall thickness, LV and RV chamber size, LV mass, systolic function, and apical aneurysms. LGE indicates higher cardiac arrest risk due to ventricular tachyarrhythmias and can guide therapy decisions, especially when echocardiography results are ambiguous.
Recommendations: CMR imaging is critical for suspected HCM when cardiac symptoms, an abnormal 12-lead ECG, or family history are present, especially when standard tests are inconclusive.
Differences in LVH patterns and LGE help distinguish HCM from other conditions like storage diseases or infiltrative cardiomyopathies.
LGE is linked with higher risk for ventricular arrhythmias and HF symptoms, aiding in ICD therapy decisions.
CMR can characterize LVOT anatomy for potential septal myectomy or alcohol ablation.
CMR aids in managing high-risk morphologic features, helping prevent SCD and guiding ICD therapy.

Cardiac CT.Recommendation: In adult patients with suspected HCM, cardiac CT may be considered for diagnosis if the echocardiogram is not diagnostic and CMR imaging is unavailable.
Uses: Clear definition of LV structure including hypertrophy pattern, wall thickness measurement, detection of subaortic membrane, and intracardiac thrombus
Assessment of coronary anatomy including stenosis and anomalies
Disadvantages: Inferior temporal resolution compared with echocardiography

Recommendations: Use of 12-lead ECG in patients with HCM for initial evaluation and periodic follow-up.
Ambulatory electrocardiographic monitoring (24- to 48-hour) in patients with HCM to detect arrhythmias.
Extended (>24 hours) electrocardiographic monitoring for patients with HCM experiencing palpitations or lightheadedness.
12-lead ECG for first-degree relatives of patients with HCM.
Extended ambulatory monitoring for patients at high risk of AF.
Consider extended ambulatory monitoring in adult patients without risk factors for AF who are anticoagulation-eligible.

content.Synopsis: Both 12-lead electrocardiographic and ambulatory monitoring are necessary for patients with HCM. A 12-lead ECG can provide information about LVH and repolarization abnormalities, and arrhythmias like bradycardia and tachycardia. Ambulatory monitoring is important for evaluating SCD risk. Extended monitoring helps determine the cause of symptoms or diagnose AF, especially in patients with risk factors.
content.Clinical Studies: Broad spectrum of arrhythmias in HCM patients, correlation of symptoms with monitor findings is vital.
content.Screening: ECGs are standard for initial screening of relatives with HCM. Electrocardiographic abnormalities can indicate LVH development in gene carriers.
Recommendation-Specific Supportive Text: 12-lead ECG abnormal in 75%-95% of HCM patients and helps identify phenocopies like atypical patterns.
Ambulatory monitoring is valuable for ventricular tachyarrhythmias like NSVT.
Extended ambulatory monitoring advised in patients with symptoms.
AF linked with adverse outcomes in HCM; screening and intervention developed via HCM-AF score.

recommendations.text: For patients with symptomatic HCM for whom there is uncertainty regarding the presence or severity of LVOTO on noninvasive imaging studies, invasive hemodynamic assessment with cardiac catheterization is recommended.
In patients with HCM who have symptoms or evidence of myocardial ischemia, coronary angiography (CT or invasive) is recommended.
In patients with HCM who are at risk of coronary atherosclerosis, coronary angiography (CT or invasive) is recommended before surgical myectomy.

content.synopsis: Echocardiography remains the gold standard for assessing dynamic outflow tract obstruction in HCM. Invasive hemodynamic assessment should be undertaken only when diagnostic information cannot be obtained from clinical and noninvasive imaging. This assessment is crucial for patients with persistent symptoms to fully characterize hemodynamic profile and LVOTO presence or severity. Outflow gradients can vary, influenced by myocardial contractility and loading conditions, thus provocative maneuvers are necessary to identify latent gradients. Coronary angiography aids in evaluating coronary atherosclerosis and significant myocardial bridging, informing surgical decisions.
contexts: Outflow gradients can be dynamic with variability influenced by altered myocardial contractility.
Chest discomfort is common in HCM patients due to atherosclerotic coronary risk factors.
Coronary angiography is normal in those who need surgical myectomy to address coronary atherosclerosis.

recommendations.recommendation: For symptomatic patients with HCM who do not have resting or provocable outflow tract peak gradient ≥50 mm Hg on TTE, exercise TTE is recommended for the detection and quantification of dynamic LVOTO.

In patients with nonobstructive HCM and advanced HF (NYHA functional class III to class IV), cardiopulmonary exercise stress testing should be performed to quantify the degree of functional limitation and aid in selection of patients for heart transplantation or mechanical circulatory support.
In pediatric patients with HCM, regardless of symptom status, exercise stress testing is recommended to determine functional capacity and to provide prognostic information.
In adult patients with HCM, exercise stress testing is reasonable to determine functional capacity and to provide prognostic information as part of initial evaluation.

For asymptomatic patients with HCM who do not have a resting or provocable outflow tract peak gradient ≥50 mm Hg on standard TTE, exercise TTE is reasonable for the detection and quantification of dynamic LVOTO.
In patients with obstructive HCM and ambiguous functional capacity, exercise stress testing may be reasonable to guide therapy.
In patients with HCM for whom it is unclear if their functional capacity has declined, exercise stress testing may be considered every 2 to 3 years.

content.synopsis: In patients with HCM, exercise stress testing is safe and provides information on the severity and mechanism of functional limitation. It is especially useful in predicting adverse events, including death, HF, and ventricular arrhythmias. It helps assess functional capacity and guides therapy decisions.
content.recommendation_specific_supportive_text: Stress echocardiography can be useful particularly if the patient shows symptoms after meals. CPET is a standard part of evaluation for patients with severe symptoms. In pediatric patients, a strong association of exercise-induced changes with abnormal blood pressure response exists.

Recommendations for Genetics and Family.Recommendation: In patients with HCM, evaluation of familial inheritance, including a 3-generation family history, is recommended as part of the initial assessment.
In patients with HCM, genetic testing is beneficial to elucidate the genetic basis to facilitate the identification of family members at risk for developing HCM (cascade testing).

In patients with an atypical clinical presentation of HCM or when another genetic condition is suspected to be the cause, a workup including genetic testing for HCM and other genetic causes of unexplained cardiac hypertrophy is recommended.

In patients with HCM, genetic counseling by an expert in the genetics of cardiovascular disease is recommended so that risks, benefits, test results, and their clinical significance can be reviewed and discussed with the patient in a shared decision-making process.
When performing genetic testing in a proband with HCM, the initial tier of genes tested should include genes with strong evidence to be disease-causing in HCM.
In first-degree relatives of patients with HCM, both clinical screening and cascade genetic testing (when a pathogenic/likely pathogenic variant has been identified in the proband) should be offered.

In families where a sudden unexplained death has occurred with a postmortem diagnosis of HCM, postmortem genetic testing is beneficial to facilitate cascade genetic testing and clinical screening in first-degree relatives.

In patients with HCM who have undergone genetic testing, serial reevaluation of the clinical significance of the variant(s) identified is recommended to assess for variant reclassification, which may impact diagnosis and cascade genetic testing in family members.
In affected families with HCM, preconception and prenatal reproductive and genetic counseling should be offered.

content.Synopsis: Genetic testing plays a crucial role in diagnosing and managing hypertrophic cardiomyopathy (HCM) in patients and families. HCM is inherited, with offspring having a 50% chance of inheriting the genetic variant. A thorough family history assessment and genetic testing should form a part of the clinical management of patients with HCM. Genetic assessment should be conducted in specialized centers.
Recommendation-Specific Supportive Text: Family history collection assists in identifying affected and at-risk family members.
Genetic testing is crucial for confirming diagnosis, cascade testing in the family, and guiding reproductive decisions.
Genes related to HCM can be included in first-tier testing if clinical suspicion of related systemic disorders exists.
Genetic counseling is essential to help patients understand the implications of genetic findings.

HCM primarily affects the sarcomere. Genetic panels can detect disease-causing variants in 30% of sporadic and 60% of familial cases, but only 10% of patients show a core sarcomeric variant in the absence of family history.

content.genetic_testing: Genetic testing is crucial in managing Hypertrophic Cardiomyopathy (HCM). It helps in identifying clinically actionable results which offer diagnostic clarification for the proband and cascade testing for at-risk family members.
content.cascade_testing: Upon identifying a pathogenic or likely pathogenic variant, cascade testing in family members can be initiated to find at-risk individuals for further lifelong clinical surveillance. Testing is also recommended for sarcomere gene variants, which are associated with more severe disease manifestations.
content.postmortem_testing: This can be useful if a family variant is suspected but unknown, and it may guide cascade genetic testing.
content.frequency_of_testing: Approximately 11% of variants can be reclassified over time, affecting the clinical management of family members.
content.autosomal_dominant_inheritance: HCM is inherited in an autosomal dominant pattern with a 50% chance of passing it to offspring.
content.counseling: Genetic counseling is advised to explain risks, especially related to reproductive options and disease transmission.

genes.context: Sarcomere variants are associated with more severe disease, including earlier onset, higher incidence of SCD, and arrhythmias. Patients with such variants have worse prognosis compared to those without.

content.guideline_overview: The guideline addresses the evaluation and testing protocol for individuals with suspected or family history of Hypertrophic Cardiomyopathy (HCM).
key_points: A Variant of Uncertain Significance (VUS) in a proband is not actionable but may warrant family member testing in certain research or clinical settings to further clarify pathogenicity.
If genetic testing does not identify a pathogenic variant in HCM patients, further genetic testing in family members is not indicated.
For individuals with genotype-positive but phenotype-negative HCM, no follow-up is required unless new information arises that changes the pathogenicity classification of a variant.

phenotype_negative: If the family has a known pathogenic or likely pathogenic (P/LP) variant, further testing proceeds.
If not, and no family variant is known, further clinical or genetic testing is not recommended.
phenotype_positive: Complete baseline evaluation including SCD risk and stress testing.
Regular evaluations every 1-2 years or with symptom changes, and periodic CMR every 3-5 years for SCD risk assessment.

content.description: This clinical guideline outlines the genetic testing process in hypertrophic cardiomyopathy (HCM) and provides recommendations for individuals who are genotype-positive but phenotype-negative.
HCM Genetic Testing Process: If a disease-causing Likely Pathogenic/Pathogenic (LP/P) variant is identified, cascade genetic testing in the family is recommended.
Variants can be reclassified as LP/P or as a Variant of Unknown Significance (VUS) or Likely Benign/Benign (LB/B).
Regular reevaluation for variant reclassification is advised.
If VUS, LB/B, or no variant is identified, consider second-tier testing and clinical surveillance in the family.
Genotype-Positive, Phenotype-Negative Re: Serial clinical assessment, electrocardiography, and cardiac imaging are recommended at periodic intervals (1-2 years for children/adolescents and every 3-5 years for adults).
Participation in competitive sports is considered reasonable.
Implantable Cardioverter Defibrillator (ICD) is not recommended for primary prevention.

Flowchart Details.Initial Testing: HCM Index Case Targeted Gene Testing
Flowchart Details.Positive Variant: Cascade testing and regular follow-ups are advised.
Flowchart Details.Negative Variant: Further clinical or genetic testing is not recommended.

content.Synopsis: Genotype-positive, phenotype-negative individuals are those who carry a pathogenic or likely pathogenic HCM-causing variant but are asymptomatic without evidence of LVH on cardiac imaging. These individuals are also described as having preclinical HCM. They need ongoing cardiac surveillance for development of clinical HCM.
content.Clinical Features: Reported alterations in myocardial strain, relaxation abnormalities, myocardial crypts, mitral valve leaflet abnormalities, abnormal trabeculae, myocardial scarring, electrocardiographic abnormalities, and abnormal serum NT-proBNP concentrations.
content.Screening Recommendations: Ongoing screening of genotype-positive, phenotype-negative family members of all ages is important. Screening can be less frequent in individuals over >18 years of age. Preclinical screening recommended because disease can manifest at any age.
content.Sudden Death: Sudden death in genotype-positive, phenotype-negative individuals is rare. No accurate risk prediction models for SCD exist in genotype-positive, phenotype-negative individuals currently.
content.ICD Recommendations: ICDs are not offered for primary prevention in genotype-positive, phenotype-negative individuals due to low risk of SCD. Preemptive medical therapy is not typically offered.
content.SCD Risk Assessment: Systematic noninvasive SCD risk assessment is recommended every 1 to 2 years for adults with HCM, including evaluating cardiac arrest history, syncope, family history, LV wall thickness, and ECG monitoring.

genes.context: Reported alterations include abnormal serum NT-proBNP concentrations even in the absence of LVH.

content.Synopsis: Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy (HCM) is a common cause of sudden cardiac death (SCD) in young people. Younger patients with HCM are at higher risk for SCD than older patients. No sex- or race-based differences in SCD risk have been noted. Risk evaluation involves identifying clinical markers to stratify patients for SCD prevention with implantable cardioverter-defibrillators (ICDs). The use of ICDs and periodic reassessment of SCD risk are crucial in the long-term evaluation of patients with HCM.
Recommendation-Specific Supportive Text: Observational studies in patients with HCM have identified risk factors for life-threatening ventricular arrhythmias. Initial and follow-up SCD risk assessments are crucial.
SCD risk assessment involves evaluating history of cardiac arrest, family history, ambulatory monitoring for arrhythmias, and syncope.
Cardiac magnetic resonance (CMR) imaging can help assess risk by measuring maximal LV wall thickness, apical aneurysm, and myocardial fibrosis.
Estimated 5-year SCD risk is calculated using echocardiographic measurements. The impact of newer markers like apical aneurysm and late gadolinium enhancement (LGE) on 5-year risk is undetermined.
guidelines: For patients not at high risk for SCD, further evaluation includes family history, echocardiography, and ambulatory monitoring. CMR imaging is beneficial for assessing LV wall thickness, aneurysm, and fibrosis.
For patients aged 16 and older, echocardiographic measurements including atrial diameter and gradient help calculate SCD risk.

Recommendations: For children and adolescents with HCM, a comprehensive, systematic noninvasive SCD risk assessment should be conducted initially and every 1 to 2 years.
Assessment should include personal history of cardiac arrest or sustained ventricular arrhythmias and syncope.
Consider family history of premature HCM-related SCD, minimal LV wall thickness, EF, LV apical aneurysm.
Evaluate episodes of NSVT on continuous ambulatory ECG monitoring.
In children with HCM who have a borderline risk for SCD, decisions regarding ICD placement remain uncertain and require comprehensive clinical assessment.
For patients under 16 with HCM, it is reasonable to calculate an estimated 5-year SCD risk.
Risk calculation should include echo parameters, interventricular septal thickness, LV posterior wall thickness, left atrial diameter, and genotype analysis.

genes.context: References to HCM in context of SCD risk assessment and genotype status as a risk factor.

content.general: The guideline discusses recommendations for the assessment and management of patients with Hypertrophic Cardiomyopathy (HCM). It includes criteria and methods for assessing sudden cardiac death (SCD) risk and considerations for implantable cardioverter-defibrillator (ICD) placement. Risk assessment should consider individual clinical judgment, family history, and genetic testing where applicable.

recommendations.details: In patients with HCM, individual clinical judgment is recommended when assessing the prognostic strength of conventional risk markers, engaging the patient in ICD decision-making.
ICD placement is recommended for patients with HCM and previous sustained VT or documented cardiac arrest.
Reasonable to offer ICD to adult patients with HCM and major risk factors such as family history of early HCM-related SCD, massive LVH, apical aneurysm, syncope, or LVSD.
Children with HCM having ≥1 conventional risk factors should consider ICD placement given the long-term risk of complications.

risk_factors: Family history of sudden death or SCD related to HCM in relatives ≤50 years old.
Massive left ventricular hypertrophy (LVH ≥30 mm in any LV segment).
Presence of LV apical aneurysm or transmural scar/LGE.

content.Guideline Focus: The clinical guidelines focus on risk stratification and selection of patients with hypertrophic cardiomyopathy (HCM) for implantable cardioverter-defibrillator (ICD) therapy, both primary and secondary prevention.

Risk Factors and Recommendations.Guideline: For patients with HCM with ≥1 major sudden cardiac death (SCD) risk factors, discussing estimated 5-year SCD risk and mortality rates can be useful in decision-making for ICD placement.

In select adult patients with HCM and without major SCD risk factors where ICD placement is uncertain, it may be considered if there is extensive late gadolinium enhancement (LGE) by contrast-enhanced cardiac magnetic resonance (CMR) imaging or NSVT present on monitoring.
For pediatric patients, considering factors like extensive LGE and systolic dysfunction in risk stratification can be useful in decision-making for ICD placement.
For patients with HCM without risk factors, ICD placement should not be performed.
ICD placement should not be done solely for competitive athletics participation.

content.Synopsis: Risk stratification and management in HCM involves identifying major risk factors for SCD. Recommendations emphasize shared decision making, with the understanding that individual patients’ circumstances may impact SCD risk estimates and management decisions.

content.Title: 2024 Hypertrophic Cardiomyopathy Guideline
General Information: Risk estimate scores for pediatric patients, including left atrial diameter z-score and genotype status, have been developed.
An emphasis on psychosocial impacts and long-term device complications in young patients is noted.
Association of extensive LGE with increased risk for ventricular arrhythmias in adults.
SCD risk and LGE relationship in children with HCM is not well understood.
Extended monitoring for risk stratification remains uncertain in HCM.
Considerations for device therapy and ICD placement should follow evidence of risk.
Specific Points: The complexity of ICD decision-making in young patients needs careful consideration.
5-year SCD risk calculations are emphasized for patients with major risk factors.
There is ongoing debate about the best methods for quantifying LGE.
Device therapy should avoid exposing patients to unnecessary risks.

recommendations.recommendation: In patients with HCM who are receiving an ICD, either a single-chamber transvenous ICD or a subcutaneous ICD is recommended after a shared decision-making discussion that takes into consideration patient preferences, age, lifestyle, and potential need for pacing for bradycardia or VT termination.
In patients with HCM who are receiving a transvenous ICD, single-coil ICD leads are recommended in preference to dual-coil leads, if defibrillation threshold is deemed adequate.

no_prior_event.extensive_lge_on_cmr: late gadolinium enhancement on cardiac magnetic resonance

recommendations.details: In patients with HCM who are receiving an ICD, dual-chamber ICDs are reasonable for patients with a need for atrial or atrioventricular sequential pacing for bradycardia/conduction abnormalities, or as an attempt to relieve symptoms of obstructive HCM (most commonly in patients >65 years of age).

In selected adult patients with nonobstructive HCM receiving an ICD who have NYHA class II to ambulatory class IV HF, left bundle branch block (LBBB), and LVEF <50%, cardiac resynchronization therapy (CRT) for symptom reduction is reasonable.

In patients with HCM in whom a decision has been made for ICD implantation and who have paroxysmal atrial tachycardias or AF, dual-chamber ICDs may be reasonable, but this decision must be balanced against higher complication rates of dual-chamber devices.

considerations: The decision of which type of ICD to implant is nuanced with risks and benefits.
Considerations include transvenous versus subcutaneous ICD, single-chamber versus dual-chamber vs CRT devices, and defibrillation coils.
ICD implantation in children raises additional concerns, primarily related to higher baseline heart rates and increased risk of lead fracture.
Transvenous leads have higher failure rates, while subcutaneous ICDs might pose higher complication risks for smaller individuals.
Benefits of transvenous devices include pacing ability for bradycardia and reduced size, whereas subcutaneous ICD advantages are lack of a transvenous lead and fewer lead failures.
Shared decision-making should incorporate patient preferences, lifestyle, and risk factors, especially for the need for bradycardia or VT termination pacing.

content.threshold_testing_patients: Threshold testing in patients with HCM and single-coil leads, right-sided implants, or massive hypertrophy.
content.dual_chamber_pacing_hcm: In patients with HCM needing atrial pacing, dual-chamber system might be needed; shown to reduce LVOT gradients but long-term benefits unclear.
content.crt_benefit_in_hcm: CRT benefits for HCM patients with functional class II, IVHF, LVEF ≤35%, and LBBB; small series show improvement in NYHA class or LV remodeling.
content.atrial_lead_arrhythmias: Atrial lead may help differentiate between ventricular and supraventricular arrhythmias.
content.pediatric_dual_chamber_pacing: For pediatric patients with tachyarrhythmias, dual-chamber devices may help in distinguishing VT from SVT.

pharmacological_management: Nonvasodilating beta blockers titrated to effectiveness.
Nondihydropyridine calcium channel blockers (verapamil, diltiazem) if beta blockers ineffective.
Addition of myosin inhibitor for persistent symptoms despite medication.
Intravenous phenylephrine for acute hypotension not responding to fluids.
Low-dose oral diuretics for persistent dyspnea with volume overload.

content.guideline: Treatment guidelines for obstructive Hypertrophic Cardiomyopathy (HCM) focus on symptom relief and managing outflow tract obstruction.

medications.beta_blockers: First-line treatment for obstructive HCM; titrated to dose achieving symptom benefit.
medications.calcium_channel_blockers: Verapamil and diltiazem as alternatives to beta blockers.
medications.vasodilators: Discontinuation recommended due to potential worsening of symptoms.
medications.advanced_therapies: Disopyramide, mavacamten, and septal reduction therapy (SRT) for refractory cases.

content.precautions: Verapamil and diltiazem have potential vasodilating properties that may limit use in certain patients.

content.Severe and progressive pulmonary hyperte: Patient selection and treatment strategies for obstructive HCM include determining the appropriateness of GDMT, SRT, or surgical myectomy. Surgical options are recommended for patients with severe symptoms or in those for whom surgery is contraindicated. Sub-sections include specific surgical indicators like severe pulmonary hypertension, left atrial enlargement, and elevated LVOT gradients.

content.SRT: Surgical Risk Treatment (SRT) is recommended for drug-refractory symptoms and should be performed in experienced HCM centers. It is not recommended for patients with HCM who are asymptomatic and have normal exercise capacity. Septal reduction therapies (SRTs) include septic myectomy and alcohol septal ablation.
content.ESM: Transaortic extended septal myectomy (ESM) is preferred for a broad range of patients and helps in reducing SAM-mediated MR and its consequences. It is advantageous for long-term survival and reduction of obstruction.
content.Alcohol Septal Ablation: Considered less effective with lower outcomes compared to ESM. It is associated with a low procedural mortality rate (<1%) but requires sophisticated coronary anatomy.

Recommendations.SRT for Obstructive HCM: Recommended for those with symptoms refractory to medication. Guidelines suggest not performing procedures solely to reduce risk of LVOTO if asymptomatic.

Key Points: Surgical myectomy can improve outcomes such as pulmonary hypertension and left atrial enlargement.
Recommendation for surgical intervention limited to comprehensive HCM centers due to high success rates and low complication rates.
Alternative options like septal reduction therapy (SRT) discussed for severe cases.
Mitral valve replacement considered in specific cases when necessary.
Management of patients with nonobstructive HCM and preserved ejection fraction (EF) using beta blockers or calcium channel blockers.
Management Recommendations: Beta blockers or nondihydropyridine calcium channel blockers for nonobstructive HCM with preserved EF.
Use of oral diuretics for exertional dyspnea.
Angiotensin-converting enzyme inhibitors and angiotensin receptor blockers useful in managing symptoms.
Consider apical myectomy in selected cases with severe symptoms despite medical therapy.

content.Synopsis: Symptomatic, nonobstructive HCM is a diagnostic and therapeutic challenge. It is related to differences in disease onset, severity, and risk for adverse outcomes. The overall risk for HCM-related death appears similar between patients with and without obstructive physiology. Dyspnea and chest discomfort are common symptoms. These can result from increased LV filling pressures related to diastolic dysfunction or decompensated HF. Control of comorbid conditions can provide symptom reduction.
Recommendation-Specific Supportive Text: Pharmacological management of chest discomfort is similar to that of dyspnea. Beta blockers and nonhydropyridine calcium channel blockers are first-line agents.
Loop or thiazide diuretics may be used to improve dyspnea and volume overload when volume overload is present.
Despite pilot trials, no benefit was seen with losartan in preventing clinical outcomes.
Patients with extensive apical hypertrophy may require surgical interventions like transapical myectomy.

genes.context: For younger (e.g., ≤45 years of age) patients with nonobstructive HCM due to a pathogenic or likely pathogenic cardiac sarcomere genetic variant, and a mild phenotype, valsartan may be beneficial to slow adverse cardiac remodeling.

content.General Information: The guideline discusses management of patients with hypertrophic cardiomyopathy (HCM) and advanced heart failure (HF). Focus is on using beta blockers, calcium channel blockers, and valsartan to manage symptoms. Recommendations are provided for patients with systolic dysfunction, persistent LV dysfunction, and those needing heart transplantation.

Recommendations.Details: In patients with HCM who develop systolic dysfunction with an LVEF <50%, guideline-directed medical therapy for HF with reduced EF is recommended.
Diagnostic testing is recommended to assess for causes of systolic dysfunction, such as coronary artery disease (CAD).
In nonobstructive HCM and advanced HF, CPET is recommended to gauge functional limitation and aid in selection for heart transplantation.
For those with nonobstructive HCM or life-threatening arrhythmias refractory to maximal GDMT, heart transplantation assessment is recommended.
Discontinue cardiac myosin inhibitors in patients with LVEF <50% and persistent systolic dysfunction.
Discontinuation of negative inotropic agents is reasonable for patients with LVEF <50%.
For transplant candidates, continuous-flow LVAD therapy is reasonable as a bridge to heart transplantation.
ICD placement can be beneficial for patients with persistent LVEF <50%.
CRT can improve symptoms in patients with LVEF <50% and NYHA class II to IV symptoms.

genes.context: In patients with nonobstructive HCM, pathogenic or likely pathogenic sarcomeric variants are considered.

content.Summary: An approach to the management of heart failure (HF) symptoms in patients with hypertrophic cardiomyopathy (HCM) is discussed. Ejection fraction (EF) <50% indicates severe systolic dysfunction, warranting specific therapies. Despite the infrequency of reduced EF in HCM, patients with EF <50% may have increased mortality risk, necessitating HF therapies.
content.HF Therapy in HCM: Guideline directed management and therapy (GDMT) is initiated for HCM patients with reduced EF <50%. Advanced HF therapies are considered with EF <35%.
content.Transplantation Considerations: Transplantation referral should follow specific guidelines. Patients with preserved EF yet restrictive physiology might not require reduced EF for transplant referral.
content.Medications: Certain medications such as mavacamten are noted for efficacy in this group. Consideration is needed for drugs like negative inotropes (verapamil, diltiazem, disopyramide) disused if worsening HF.
content.Exercise and Assessment: Exercise parameters assist in evaluation. Cardiopulmonary exercise testing is useful.

genes.context: Myocardial transcriptomic profiling has found significant overlap in gene network activation between dilated cardiomyopathy and HCM, including MYBPC3 mutations.

content.Title: Management of Symptoms in Patients With HCM
Steps: Determine if there is obstructive physiology: If no, refer to another figure; if yes, check for symptoms.
If symptoms are present, avoid vasodilators and high-dose diuretics.
Prescribe beta blocker, verapamil, or diltiazem.
If symptoms persist, options include myosin inhibitor, disopyramide, or septal reduction therapy.
Consider septal ablation or myectomy based on surgical indication.
content.Notes: Colors correspond to Table 3. GL indicates guideline; HCM stands for hypertrophic cardiomyopathy.

content.Overview: The guideline presents a decision-making algorithm for patients with hypertrophic cardiomyopathy (HCM), focusing on heart failure management depending on the presence of obstructive physiology and the level of left ventricular ejection fraction (LVEF). Treatment involves guideline-directed management, evaluation for alternative causes of heart failure, and discontinuation of certain medications based on clinical findings.

Steps in Algorithm.Action: Refer to the section on obstructive HCM.

Actions: Follow GDMT per Heart Failure Guideline.
Evaluate for other causes of reduced EF.
Discontinue negative inotropic agents (verapamil, diltiazem, disopyramide).
Consider Implantable Cardioverter-Defibrillator (ICD) for certain cases.
Reevaluation after GDMT for recurrent ventricular arrhythmias.

Steps in Algorithm.NYHA Class III-IV: Evaluate for heart transplant if LVEF <50% and LBBB is present, or LVAD if patient decompensates.

Additional Notes: Cardiac myosin inhibitor should be discontinued if LVEF <50% and can be restarted at a lower dose if LVEF recovers.
CRT is recommended if LVEF <50% and LBBB is present.

content.focus: Management of Patients with Hypertrophic Cardiomyopathy (HCM) and Atrial Fibrillation (AF)

recommendations_summary.recommendation: In patients with HCM and clinical AF, anticoagulation is recommended with DOACs as first-line option and vitamin K antagonists as second-line option.
In patients with HCM and subclinical AF detected by longer monitoring, anticoagulation is recommended with DOACs as first-line option.
In patients with AF in whom rate control strategy is planned, beta blockers, verapamil, or diltiazem are recommended.
In patients with HCM and subclinical AF of >5 minutes duration, anticoagulation with DOACs as first-line and vitamin K antagonists as second-line option can be beneficial.
In patients with HCM and poorly tolerated AF, a rhythm-control strategy with cardioversion or antiarrhythmic drugs can be beneficial.
In patients with HCM and symptomatic AF, catheter ablation for AF can be effective.
In patients with HCM and AF who require surgical myectomy, concomitant surgical AF ablation can be beneficial for AF rhythm control.

content.Synopsis: AF, commonly observed in patients with HCM, is associated with significant morbidity and an increased risk of stroke. Therapy includes prevention of thromboembolic events and symptom control. Use of DOACs and anticoagulation is recommended, considering the duration of AF episodes and underlying risk factors. Catheter ablation is less effective than in the general population. Surgical AF ablation might be an option during surgical myectomy. Diagnosis and management are complex, considering factors like total AF burden and presence of risk factors.

Recommendation-Specific Supportive Text.1: Clinical AF in patients can be managed hand through rhythm-control or rate-control strategies, considering patient-specific factors. A meta-analysis showed a prevalence of 27.09% for thromboembolism in patients with HCM with AF, with a 3.75 per 10 patient risk. The stroke risk is independent of CHA2DS2-VASc Score, emphasizing anticoagulation benefits. DOACs are effective with additional advantages. Recommendations for patients with AF atrial flutter are similar to those without HCM.
Recommendation-Specific Supportive Text.2: Management of clinical and asymptomatic or subclinical AF (SCAF) is crucial due to increased stroke risk.
Recommendation-Specific Supportive Text.3: Rate-control strategy may involve nonhydropyridine calcium channel blocker, beta-blocker, or a combination. The choice depends on age, underlying substrate, coexisting conditions, and severity of symptoms.
Recommendation-Specific Supportive Text.4: SCAF is often observed in patients with implanted cardiac devices and HCM. The risk threshold for anticoagulation is still debated, though studies suggest episodes over 24 hours correlate with increased risk.

content.Title: Antiarrhythmic Drug Therapy Options for Patients With HCM and AF

Drugs and Usage.Use in HCM: Particularly with early onset AF. Generally used in conjunction with atrioventricular nodal blocking agents.
Not generally recommended in the absence of an ICD.

Drugs and Usage.Toxicities: Liver, lung, thyroid, skin, neurologic, Prolonged QTc

Additional Information: AF indicates atrial fibrillation; HCM, hypertrophic cardiomyopathy; HF, heart failure; ICD, implantable cardioverter-defibrillator; and TdP, torsades de pointes.
Increased stroke risk is associated with AF in HCM.
Catheter ablation is an important role in management of AF in HCM even though RCTs do not exist.
Low mortality and morbidity compared with historical controls when treating AF in patients with HCM.
Disopyramide is safely prescribed for reduction of LVOTO.

content.Title: 2024 Hypertrophic Cardiomyopathy Guideline
content.Published In: Journal of the American College of Cardiology (JACC), Vol. 83, No. 23
content.Topic: Management of Patients with Hypertrophic Cardiomyopathy (HCM) and Ventricular Arrhythmias
Key Points: In patients with HCM and recurrent, poorly tolerated life-threatening ventricular tachyarrhythmias refractory to maximal antiarrhythmic drug therapy and ablation, heart transplantation assessment is indicated.

For adults with HCM and symptomatic ventricular arrhythmias or recurrent ICD shocks despite beta-blocker use, antiarrhythmic drug therapy is recommended with careful selection based on patient-specific factors.
Children with HCM and recurrent ventricular arrhythmias despite beta-blocker use should have antiarrhythmic drug therapy guided by age, patient preferences, and balance of efficacy and safety.
In patients with HCM and pacing-capable ICDs, programming to minimize risk of shocks is advised.
In patients with HCM and recurrent symptomatic sustained monomorphic VT, catheter ablation can be useful when antiarrhythmic drugs are not preferred.
content.Research Context: Study outcomes related to the use of ICD therapy and antiarrhythmic drugs in managing HCM.

content.title: 2024 Hypertrophic Cardiomyopathy Guideline

sections.title: Pediatric Patients with HCM
sections.description: Recurrent episodes of VT in pediatric patients with HCM are generally treated with beta blockers. Antiarrhythmic agents may be used to address symptoms, suppress life-threatening events, or prevent unnecessary ICD shocks. ICD shocks have been linked to psychological trauma in pediatric patients. Sympathetic denervation has been reported with limited data.

sections.title: Patient Management
sections.description: In patients with HCM and recurrent ventricular arrhythmias, additional therapies like epicardial ablation may be required. Freedom from VT was found in patients post-ablation. Combined epicardial and endocardial ablation is safe for treating monomorphic VT.

sections.title: Lifestyle Considerations for Patients with HCM
sections.description: Recreational physical activity is beneficial for patients with mild-to-moderate intensity exercise to improve fitness and quality of life. Comprehensive evaluation is recommended for athletes with HCM prior to competitive sports participation.

guidelines: For patients with HCM, engaging in mild-to-moderate intensity exercise is beneficial for overall health.
Athletes with HCM should undergo a comprehensive evaluation before participating in sports.
Individuals who are genotype-positive, phenotype-negative for HCM can participate in competitive sports.
Participation in vigorous recreational activities is reasonable with professional evaluation.

genes.context: Individuals who are genotype-positive, phenotype-negative for HCM, participation in competitive sports of any intensity is reasonable. In pediatric patients, age and heart size must be taken into account when considering ablation.

content.guideline_summary: The document provides guidelines for patients with hypertrophic cardiomyopathy (HCM) regarding physical activity and sports participation. It discusses the benefits of regular physical exercise in reducing cardiovascular risk, while emphasizing the importance of individualized evaluation before participation in vigorous activities. Patients with HCM can consider competitive sports after expert evaluation if they have no significant symptomatic limitation. Universal restriction from vigorous activities is not necessary for most patients with HCM. Specific considerations are given for individuals with an implantable cardioverter-defibrillator (ICD).

recommendations.summary: Participation in competitive sports may be considered for patients with HCM with a high level of physical performance after expert review.
Universal restriction from vigorous physical activity or competitive sports is not indicated for most patients with HCM.
ICD placement for the sole purpose of competitive sports participation should not be performed.

key_points: Regular physical activity promotes longevity and reduces cardiovascular risk.
Most patients with HCM benefit from at least mild- to moderate-intensity exercise.
Careful, individualized evaluation is required before engaging in vigorous exercise or competitive sports.
Genotype-positive patients with negative phenotypic expression show low risk of sudden cardiac death.
Exercise intensity can be managed by metabolic equivalents with a structured exercise plan.

content.HCM Patients and Exercise: Many patients with HCM who have no or minimal symptomatic limitation can engage in vigorous-intensity exercise. Studies do not show an increased risk of ventricular arrhythmias from exercise. Competitive athletes with HCM did not experience increased arrhythmic risks compared to those who exercise less vigorously.
content.Risk Assessment: Annual comprehensive clinical evaluation and risk assessment should be individualized with shared decision-making for HCM patients considering vigorous exercise.
content.ICD Placement: Risk stratification and recommendations for ICD should follow the guideline's algorithm. Inappropriate ICD use should be avoided to prevent unnecessary anxiety for HCM patients.
content.Occupation Recommendations for HCM Patie: HCM patients who demonstrate high physical performance may consider certain occupations after comprehensive evaluation and risk assessment for SCD.

content.Synopsis: Several occupational considerations are important for patients with HCM, particularly when potential for loss of consciousness could occur. For some occupations, federal guidelines cannot be superseded by this guideline.
Recommendation-Specific Supportive Text: The Federal Motor Carrier Safety Administration guidelines updated in 2015 allow driving commercial vehicles for those with HCM who do not have an ICD or major SCD risk factors.
FAA guidelines do not explicitly list HCM as disqualifying for piloting aircraft, but report suggests patients with asymptomatic HCM may be considered for flying duties.
Occupations requiring considerable physical labor or high physical performance may impose risk to patients with HCM and others. Decisions should be individual and context-based.
Recommendations for Pregnancy in Patient: Anticoagulation for pregnant women with HCM and AF for stroke prevention is recommended.
Beta blockers for symptom management in pregnant women with HCM are advised.
Vaginal delivery is recommended for most pregnant women with HCM.
Preconceptional and genetic counseling should be offered to affected families.
Care should be coordinated by a cardiologist and obstetrician for pregnant women with HCM.
It is advised to initiate GDMT for women with stable HCM who wish to become pregnant.
Cardioversion for new or recurrent AF in pregnant women with HCM is reasonable.
General or epidural anesthesia with precautions is reasonable.
Use of mavacamten is contraindicated due to teratogenic effects.

content.Synopsis: Pregnancy in most women with hypertrophic cardiomyopathy (HCM) is well tolerated, with very low maternal mortality. Symptoms may include dyspnea, chest pain, palpitations, and complications such as heart failure (HF) and arrhythmias. These occur in about 25% of pregnant women with HCM. Outcomes for women with left ventricular outflow tract obstruction (LVOTO) are similar to those without obstruction.
Recommendations: 1. Atrial fibrillation (AF) is associated with stroke in HCM and can be treated with anticoagulation.
2. Beta blockers (like metoprolol) are considered safe but need fetal monitoring.
3. Cardiovascular diseases including cardiomyopathies impact delivery outcomes. Both vaginal and cesarean deliveries are options.
4. Prenatal genetic counseling is advised for understanding disease transmission risks and reproductive choices.
5. A multidisciplinary team is suggested for managing pregnant women with HCM.
6. Cardiac events can occur primarily in women with pre-existing symptoms. Options include septal reduction therapy (SRT) or implantable cardioverter-defibrillators (ICD) for severe cases.
7. Antiarrhythmic agents are generally avoided during pregnancy.
8. Anesthesia management should be comfortable and cautious.
9. Echocardiography is recommended if complications arise.
10. Fetal echocardiography assists in prenatal diagnosis.
11. Myosin inhibitors may pose fetal toxicity risks.

content.summary: The guideline addresses patients with hypertrophic cardiomyopathy (HCM) who have comorbidities such as hypertension, obesity, and sleep-disordered breathing. Key recommendations include adherence to primary prevention guidelines for cardiovascular events, weight loss interventions for overweight or obese patients, and lifestyle modifications for those with hypertension. Referral for sleep medicine evaluation is also advised if necessary. Obesity and sleep-disordered breathing can increase risk in HCM patients, possibly involving sarcomere gene variants.
recommendations: Adherence to the ACC/AHA primary prevention guideline to reduce cardiovascular risk.
Weight loss counseling and lifestyle interventions for overweight/obese patients with HCM.
Lifestyle modifications and medical therapy for hypertension in HCM patients, with preference for beta blockers and non-dihydropyridine calcium channel blockers in obstructive HCM.
Assessment and referral for sleep-disordered breathing in HCM patients.
risks: Comorbid conditions such as obesity and sleep-disordered breathing increase symptom burden and risk in HCM patients.
Hypertension is prevalent in HCM patients and affects sarcomere variant-negative patients disproportionately.

genes.context: Patients with HCM and sarcomere gene variants may have increased risk for cardiovascular complications and symptom burden.

content.title: Evidence Gaps and Future Directions (Section 10)
content.summary: This section outlines limitations in current HCM diagnosis and management and highlights priorities for research, including refining diagnostic criteria, developing disease‑modifying therapies, improving care for nonobstructive HCM, expanding risk stratification, arrhythmia management, and expanding understanding of genetic architecture.

points: Diagnosis currently based on binary LV wall thickness cutoffs leading to over- and under-diagnosis due to measurement imprecision and variability (sex, body size, comorbidities).
Recommendation to evolve toward molecular or pathway-based approaches to improve diagnostic accuracy and patient stratification.
Goal of developing safe, effective medical therapy to forestall progression—existing medications (eg, valsartan) and emerging meds (eg, cardiac myosin inhibitors) are being investigated.
If specific genetic etiology identified, gene-based therapies could durably impact disease; need better understanding of pathogenesis, predictors of progression, and intermediate phenotypes.
Nonobstructive HCM remains challenging; obstructive physiology can be targeted with medical/surgical approaches, but drivers of nonobstructive disease are less clear.
Diastolic abnormalities and myocardial energetics are thought important but not well addressed; role of cardiac myosin inhibitors in nonobstructive HCM under investigation.
Existing risk stratification algorithms have low positive-predictive value; many ICDs may be placed unnecessarily while sudden cardiac arrest can occur in patients without established risk factors.
Need new risk factors, tools (including AI), and larger prospective registries with well-genotyped and phenotyped patients, especially pediatric and underrepresented groups.
Imaging markers (LGE, abnormal 3D strain) and biomarkers (eg, troponin) may help but need validation against current metrics before clinical adoption.
AF is common in adult HCM patients, often poorly tolerated and more refractory to treatment than in non-HCM patients.
Further work needed to identify robust predictors for AF, refine risk scores, and improve stratification for thromboembolic complications; technical advances in ablation may increase success rates.
Genetic evaluation and counseling are unevenly available; greater access to genetic testing and expert interpretation is needed.
Better understanding of genetic contributors could enable targeted therapies and improved patient stratification.

other_clinical_points.sleep_disordered_breathing: Highly prevalent in HCM (55–70%); obstructive sleep apnea associated with older age, hypertension, greater symptom burden, reduced exercise capacity, and greater prevalence of AF and NSVT. Treatment may reduce symptoms and arrhythmic complications but has not been systematically tested.
other_clinical_points.therapeutics_investigations: Clinical benefit shown with sodium-glucose cotransporter-2 inhibitors and mineralocorticoid receptor antagonists in HFpEF; trials exploring benefit in nonobstructive HCM are needed. Lifestyle interventions and cardiopulmonary rehabilitation may be beneficial if reimbursed for HCM.

content.summary: For patients with Hypertrophic Cardiomyopathy (HCM) to improve individual care, family management, and knowledge base, there is a need for improved algorithms for interpreting genetic variants classified as uncertain significance. Efforts include ongoing research via the Clinical Genome Resource. Approximately 50% of HCM cases are genetically elusive, requiring new gene discovery. Complex genetic understanding can advance patient stratification and management, particularly in predicting heritable diseases and the effectiveness of gene-specific therapies.

genes.context: Approximately 50% of cases of HCM are genetically elusive. Improved algorithms for the interpretation of variants that are currently classified as variants of uncertain significance are evolving.

content.scope: The guideline addresses the evaluation, diagnosis, and treatment of hypertrophic cardiomyopathy, including recent advancements and evidence-based practices.

content.Etiology: Genetic factors and shared genetic pathways contribute to hypertrophic cardiomyopathy.

content.General information: The document provides an overview of the guidelines for the diagnosis, management, and treatment of hypertrophic cardiomyopathy (HCM), with references to studies and clinical practices. It covers sections on myocardial ischemia, autonomic dysfunction, shared decision-making, and multidisciplinary care for HCM patients.

genes.context: Sarcomere protein mutations in MYBPC3 are linked to hypertrophic cardiomyopathy.

content.clinical_information: The document discusses various clinical guidelines and research findings related to hypertrophic cardiomyopathy (HCM). It includes topics such as genetic contributions to the disease, echocardiographic advancements, family screening, clinical outcomes, and the progression of the condition. The text emphasizes the importance of surgical and clinical management based on individual patient profiles and highlights the significance of current technologies in diagnosis and treatment.

content.Overview: The document provides clinical guidelines for the evaluation and management of hypertrophic cardiomyopathy (HCM). It includes information on cardiac CT, heart rhythm assessment, and invasive hemodynamic assessment.
content.Cardiac CT: Recommends the use of cardiac CT for the evaluation of patients with HCM, identifying myocardial fibrosis, and assessing left ventricular outflow tract gradients.
content.Heart Rhythm Assessment: Discusses the use of electrocardiograms (ECG) and ambulatory monitoring to diagnose and prognosticate patients with HCM. Highlights the significance of atrial fibrillation and ventricular arrhythmias.
content.Invasive Hemodynamic Assessment: Describes the assessment of left ventricular inflow and outflow tract pressures, using techniques such as iopetrolol for gradient determination.
content.Exercise Stress Testing: Evaluates exercise stress testing for diagnosing left ventricular outflow tract obstruction in HCM patients.

content.Pharmacological Management: Symptomatic patients with obstructive hypertrophic cardiomyopathy are managed using beta-adrenergic blockers like propranolol and verapamil to ameliorate angina and improve functional status. Disopyramide is also used for therapy.
content.Invasive Treatment: Involves surgical myectomy or septal ablation. Outcomes compare favorably with dedicated HCM centers managing dynamic obstruction.
content.Genetic Considerations: Valve and papillary muscle abnormalities may be present. Asymmetrical septal hypertrophy impacts survival. Septal reduction therapies are tailored based on morphological characteristics.

content.summary: The document reviews surgical and clinical interventions for patients with Hypertrophic Obstructive Cardiomyopathy. It includes studies on ventricular septal myectomy, mitral valve procedures, and alcohol septal ablation. It discusses various approaches such as transaortic and multidisciplinary surgical methodologies for patient management.
key_points: Surgical myectomy provides symptom relief and can be performed in subaortic, midventricular, and apical regions.
Mitral valve involvement and repair can influence outcomes post-myectomy.
Alcohol septal ablation is an alternative to surgical methods with varying long-term outcomes.
Improvements in life expectancy and disease management have resulted from precise surgical interventions.

content.Hypertrophic Cardiomyopathy: The guideline focuses on hypertrophic cardiomyopathy (HCM), discussing its diagnosis, symptoms, and treatment options. It mentions various interventions like implantable cardioverter-defibrillators, radio-frequency catheter ablation, and surgery.
content.Physical Activity and Sports Participati: The guideline addresses the safety of sports and physical activity in patients with HCM, suggesting that moderate exercise is beneficial. It highlights studies on exercise training programs and sports participation for individuals with HCM.
content.Sudden Cardiac Death: Several studies are cited concerning the risk factors and prevention of sudden cardiac death in individuals with HCM. The use of implantable cardioverter-defibrillators is discussed as well as other preventative strategies.
content.Pediatric Considerations: The guideline includes information specific to pediatric patients with HCM, discussing the outcomes and management strategies for younger populations.
